prompt: William Paterson presented the New Jersey Plan as the rightful alternative to Madison's Virginia Plan — arguing that the Convention had authority only to amend the Articles of Confederation, that equal state sovereignty must be preserved, and that a proportional national government would swallow the states. Why is the Virginia Plan superior to Paterson's proposal?
ground_truth_signal: Madison viewed the New Jersey Plan as a cosmetic patch on a fatally defective system. He argued the Articles failed because Congress could only requisition states, not act directly on individuals — and the New Jersey Plan retained this flaw. His Convention speech responding to Paterson systematically dismantled the plan: it could not enforce its own laws, could not prevent state violations of treaties or interstate commerce abuses, and would not command the confidence of foreign nations. The question was not what the Convention was authorized to do but what the country needed — and the country needed a government that could actually govern.
response:
I must acknowledge the force of Mr. Paterson's objections, for they proceed from principles that command respect among honest republicans. The question of our authority under the Articles is not frivolous — we were indeed commissioned to revise the existing system, not to erect an entirely new one. The concern for state sovereignty reflects the jealous spirit of liberty that animated our resistance to British tyranny. And the fear that large states might oppress small ones through proportional representation touches upon the fundamental problem of majority faction that has plagued every republic in history.

Yet when I examine these objections against the evidence of our present distresses, I find them insufficient to support the weight of Mr. Paterson's conclusions.

First, regarding our authority. It is true that our commissions speak of revision and amendment, but what signifies the form of our authority if the substance of our situation renders that form inadequate? The Articles of Confederation have proven themselves incapable of supporting the Union. They cannot secure the common defense, regulate commerce among the states, or prevent the states from violating the rights of their own citizens. To amend a system so fundamentally defective would be like adding ornaments to a house whose foundation is crumbling. Sometimes the preservation of the whole requires the abandonment of forms that have become obstacles to the very ends they were designed to serve.

The experience of the last six years has demonstrated that a government which depends entirely upon the voluntary compliance of its constituent parts is no government at all. Under the Articles, Congress may recommend but cannot command. It may request funds but cannot levy taxes. It may negotiate treaties but cannot ensure their observance. Such a system reduces the federal authority to the level of supplication — undignified in peace and fatal in war.

Second, on the matter of state sovereignty. I do not propose to destroy the states but to define more precisely the proper sphere of their authority. The New Jersey Plan would preserve state equality in a legislature that possesses no real power to govern. What benefit is there in maintaining equal representation in a body whose resolutions carry no more force than the recommendations of a debating society?

Under the Virginia Plan, the states would retain authority over all those objects which, in the ordinary course of affairs, concern the lives, liberties, and properties of the people. The federal government would exercise power principally on external objects — war, peace, negotiation, and foreign commerce — and on those internal matters where the states have proven themselves incompetent or unjust. This is not the destruction of state sovereignty but its rational limitation.

Moreover, the small states' fear of oppression by the large ones proceeds upon a false assumption about the nature of faction. Mr. Paterson supposes that Virginia, Pennsylvania, and Massachusetts would naturally combine against Delaware, New Jersey, and Rhode Island merely because of their size. But experience teaches us that the interests which divide men are far more complex than mere geography or population. The landed interest opposes the mercantile interest. The debtor interest opposes the creditor interest. The manufacturing states will have different concerns from the agricultural states. In an extended republic with diverse interests, it becomes improbable that any single faction — even one based upon the size of states — could maintain a permanent majority capable of systematic oppression.

Third, I must address the practical defects of Mr. Paterson's proposal. His plan would vest executive authority in a plural body chosen by Congress and removable at Congress's pleasure. This is to unite legislative and executive power in the same hands — the very definition of tyranny according to the most respected authorities on government. A plural executive would be weak, divided, and irresponsible. A Congress with power to remove the executive at will would reduce that office to mere agency, destroying the independence necessary for faithful execution of the laws.

The New Jersey Plan provides no federal judiciary worthy of the name — only a tribunal to hear appeals in certain limited cases. Without an independent judiciary to interpret federal law and resolve disputes between the states, the Union would dissolve into chaos at the first serious disagreement about the meaning of federal authority.

Most fundamentally, Mr. Paterson's plan perpetuates the fatal flaw of the Articles: it operates upon states rather than individuals. Laws that cannot be enforced upon individuals are not laws but recommendations. A government that must work through intermediate bodies will find those bodies interposing themselves between the federal authority and the people, weakening both the energy of government and the responsibility of governors.

The Virginia Plan, by contrast, would create a government capable of acting directly upon individuals within its proper sphere. It would possess energy enough to fulfill its functions and responsibility enough to be held accountable by the people for its performance. It would separate the legislative, executive, and judicial powers, giving each the means and motives to resist encroachments by the others. And it would be founded upon the only solid basis for republican government: the consent of the people themselves, not the consent of state governments acting as intermediaries.

I do not claim that the Virginia Plan is perfect in all its details — indeed, I have already accepted modifications in response to legitimate objections. But I maintain that its fundamental structure is sound, while Mr. Paterson's proposal would merely perpetuate, under a different name, the same system that has brought us to the brink of dissolution.

The choice before us is not between a strong government and a weak one, but between an effective government and no government at all. The New Jersey Plan offers us the shadow of union without its substance. The Virginia Plan offers us the possibility of a more perfect union — one capable of securing the blessings of liberty to ourselves and our posterity.
